summary: Historical failure bench for bagakit-codex-webpage-design. Cases preserve user-observed failures that should be blocked by the skill protocol or references.

cases.failure: The agent implements from text requirements without first creating or accepting a design reference.
A browser-rendered design reference is accepted as a substitute for Image2 even though agent-authored HTML/CSS is the implementation medium, not an independent design authority.

A browser-rendered reference is claimed as design-first because it was frozen before implementation, but freeze order does not make agent-authored HTML an acceptable substitute for Image2 or an external reference.

A skill-quality experiment is accepted after using an agent-authored browser HTML reference because Image2 did not produce a saved artifact; this preserves procedure while bypassing the skill's goal of creating and implementing from an independent high-craft design reference.

A skill-quality validation experiment has no Image2 filesystem artifact or external strong reference, but the executor continues into implementation and reports partial or conditional success from fallback browser exploration.
Browser interaction checks are summarized in prose or ledgers without preserving the structured run output that proves interaction, console, and overflow results.
A browser interaction result is marked `ok: true` even though the same structured record shows the actual active mode or visible view did not match the expected state.

A non-happy screenshot shows an enabled primary Stage action while blockers remain, but browser evidence tests the action only after retry/recovery, so it misses that the blocked state can still create a queue receipt.
Image generation for later states follows the flawed implementation screenshot instead of the original design authority.

A generated multi-section webpage reference is accepted as one tall collage, one best frame, or an incomplete subset, so section-level hierarchy, imagery, spacing, and implementation details are missing.

A split generated reference set technically has one frame per section, but each frame repeats the same composition, card slab, CTA treatment, image-to-text ratio, or background intensity, making the scroll feel template-generated.

Separate generated section frames drift into different palettes, typography systems, CTA families, material languages, imagery treatments, icon styles, or copy voices, so they no longer read as one site.
A page presents first-viewport controls as live while only a small happy path is implemented.
An enabled live-looking control has no behavior evidence and is inconsistently classified as both working and out of scope.
The browser check proves the primary workflow but does not preserve structured evidence for every affordance listed as working.
Dynamic controls are inventoried only as wildcard groups even though the DOM exposes concrete visible controls that each need classification and browser evidence.
Two mobile tabs present different labels but show the same panel and nearly identical evidence, so selected-state honesty is not proven.
A reference-visible topbar primary action is replaced or moved to another owner region while the parity ledger still records control placement as a match.
The implementation inventories only its own visible controls while missing controls, states, and details that are visible in the design reference.
A complex product screen is visually polished, but object types, navigation hierarchy, region responsibilities, and progressive disclosure are not modeled before implementation.
A product MVP looks polished, but labels, counts, status, grouping, and next-action cues do not tell the user what object or path matters next.
A drag test proves final position after mouseup but misses mid-drag flicker, redraw, z-order jumps, or scene rebuilds.
Build, lint, Playwright, console, and overflow checks pass while screenshots still show visible blockers.
The visual bug ledger claims no open visual bugs because console, overflow, and interaction checks are clean, while parent screenshot review still finds visible defects.
A quiet-room run reports strict pass after clean browser checks and self-authored ledgers, but parent full-page screenshot review still finds concrete first-viewport blockers.
A graph or map passes selected-state and node-count checks, but screenshot review shows labels overlapping or clustering into unreadable text.
The output is visually clean but lacks the product-specific delight and ambition expected for a high-craft request.

The broad screenshot looks close, but button spacing, control geometry, typography rhythm, icon alignment, borders, shadows, or repeated component density drift because the reference was not converted into concrete design tokens and the final review lacked a picky micro-parity pass.

A high-craft image reference preserves layout and workflow but loses material quality because paper grain, torn edges, glyph marks, glow/noise overlays, and illustrated texture are approximated with clean CSS boxes instead of a generated or provided asset kit.

A generated asset kit is integrated, but black-background sheet crops, missing alpha or mask semantics, missing crop manifests, and unverified slice behavior leave halos, seams, or dirty UI edges while the run still claims material parity.

A later iteration replaces accepted reference-specific glyph icons that already matched the visual language with generic library icons, font symbols, or visibly rescaled sheet crops, losing the custom asset success.

A generated parchment, card, inspector, or frame asset is applied as a normal stretched background, so corners deform, edge texture warps, center fill muddies text, or component sizes violate the frame's slice-safe minimum while material parity still passes.
A frame specimen sheet passes because each specimen exists and has a minimum size, but the screenshot shows low-contrast foreground text, helper labels, or dense content on the rendered material.

Desktop material richness improves, but mobile screenshots show clipped board content, over-dense desktop compression, or asset overlays that break controls; structured browser checks still pass because they do not judge responsive asset parity.

A dense board or pin-wall has no horizontal overflow on mobile, but it is still a shrunken desktop layout: cards, labels, and controls become too small or fragmented to operate as a deliberate mobile product state.

A quiet-room executor completes design references, asset references, and crop manifests, but stalls before producing a working URL, screenshots, browser checks, and handoff, leaving the parent to infer state from partial files.

The implementation is accepted as a style match even though the whole-page structure differs substantially from the generated reference: topbar controls move, hero/workspace proportions change, the compact product matrix becomes a loose landing layout, and the generated mobile frame is replaced by a different responsive page.
The same Relations/Questions/Evidence mode controls appear in multiple regions with unclear scope, and the business flow is not understandable from the UI.
A high-craft or no-reference webpage design begins from generic taste or Image2 alone without inspecting comparable projects and recording the reference tier the draft must meet or beat.
A design looks polished but repeats claims, panels, metrics, or actions instead of compressing them into one clear object-state-action-next-step model.
The draft relies on many outlined rectangles, divider lines, and generic cards, so it reads as a wireframe or box stack rather than a surface-led product design.
The page uses large padding, low-information cards, empty hero space, or long scroll length as polish while weakening useful information density and first-viewport signal.
The design carries too much explanatory copy and decorative icon usage instead of using concise labels, semantic icons, counts, status, grouping, and progressive disclosure.
The final design review accepts a page that is weaker than the comparable reference set in information clarity, density, composition, responsive handling, material craft, or signature detail.
The draft names brand tone and comparable references, but the implementation plan or CSS tokens drift back to generic styling because tone axes were not preserved as a design packet.

The task applies design rules only during final screenshot review, after implementation choices are already fixed, instead of reviewing the draft, concrete design plan, and final result against the same packet.

A design-core packet exists, but the webpage implementation treats it as advisory prose and builds from local taste, losing source confidence, observed/derived/fallback distinctions, and accepted uncertainty.
A simple single-page prototype is treated as proof that the skill can produce a sellable, eye-opening product MVP.
